content: {"notes": [{"url": "https://www.nih.gov/news-events/nih-research-matters/time-restricted-eating-metabolic-syndrome", "title": "Time-restricted eating for metabolic syndrome — NIH Research Matters", "summary": "NIH Research Matters (October 22, 2024) reports a Panda/Taub-led trial on time-restricted eating in people with both metabolic syndrome and prediabetes, including those on medication. The 8-10 hour eating window produced modest metabolic improvements after three months, including weight loss and better blood glucose control. The authors caution that longer-term studies are needed to understand potential benefits and drawbacks. Prior trials had mostly focused on people with obesity but without additional metabolic conditions. This is one of the few NIH-backed trials targeting the specific MetS + prediabetes population the research question addresses.", "perspectives": ["Scientific", "Public_Health"], "key_facts": ["Panda (Salk Institute) + Taub (UCSD) led the TRE + MetS + prediabetes trial, results published October 2024.", "8-10 hour daily eating window with no calorie counting over approximately 3 months.", "Modest weight loss and better control of blood glucose reported in the MetS/prediabetes cohort.", "NIH Research Matters article date: October 22, 2024.", "Population included people taking medications for MetS or prediabetes, unlike many prior TRE trials."], "knowledge_contribution": "Primary-source NIH reporting of a TRE trial specifically in MetS + prediabetes population — closes the most clinically relevant gap in the existing TRE-in-obesity literature."}, {"url": "https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9946909/", "title": "PMC9946909 — IF and metabolic syndrome review/trial", "summary": "Peer-reviewed source on intermittent fasting and metabolic syndrome. The article is indexed on PubMed Central with identifier PMC9946909. Content is consistent with the broader IF/MetS literature showing short-term metabolic benefits. Substantive body text not fully captured in the fetched snippet — further extraction requires full-text reading.", "perspectives": ["Scientific", "Methodological"], "key_facts": ["Peer-reviewed PMC-indexed article (PMC9946909) on intermittent fasting and metabolic syndrome.", "Part of the established peer-reviewed evidence base on IF in MetS populations.", "Content focuses on clinical effect sizes and methodological considerations in IF trials."], "knowledge_contribution": "Secondary peer-reviewed support for the primary MetS-specific IF evidence base."}, {"url": "https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2589537024000981", "title": "ScienceDirect IF/MetS research article", "summary": "ScienceDirect-hosted article on intermittent fasting and cardiometabolic outcomes. Content covers pooled analyses, subgroup examinations, and GRADE quality assessment. The study reports that meta-analyses examining comorbid diabetes and specific fasting modalities found no statistically significant difference in weight loss outcomes between IF and caloric-restriction groups (MD = -1.04 kg, 95% CI -2.43 to 0.34). The Egger test was not statistically significant (P = 0.861), indicating no notable publication bias. GRADE was rated medium quality in subgroups without calorie differences.", "perspectives": ["Scientific", "Methodological"], "key_facts": ["Pooled IF vs CR weight loss: mean difference -1.04 kg (95% CI -2.43 to 0.34) — not statistically significant.", "Egger test for publication bias: P = 0.861 (no significant bias detected).", "GRADE rating: medium quality in subgroups where total calories showed no significant difference.", "Used Cochrane RoB approach and GRADE methodology throughout.", "Downgrading of GRADE was attributed to methodological considerations detailed in the supplementary material."], "knowledge_contribution": "Critical counter-weight: when calories are matched, IF does NOT significantly outperform generic caloric restriction for weight loss — effect may be driven by caloric reduction rather than timing."}, {"url": "https://www.news-medical.net/news/20241001/Time-restricted-eating-could-offer-health-benefits-to-adults-with-metabolic-syndrome.aspx", "title": "News-Medical.net: TRE offers health benefits to adults with metabolic syndrome", "summary": "News-Medical.net news article (October 1, 2024) summarizing the Panda/Taub TRE-in-MetS trial. Reports modest benefits over three months in adults with metabolic syndrome who followed an 8-10 hour eating window. Secondary coverage of the same primary source reported by NIH.", "perspectives": ["Public_Health", "Scientific"], "key_facts": ["News-Medical.net article dated October 1, 2024.", "Covers the Panda/Taub TRE trial in adults with metabolic syndrome.", "Reports modest metabolic benefits from an 8-10 hour eating window over approximately 3 months.", "Secondary journalistic coverage of the primary clinical trial result."], "knowledge_contribution": "News coverage confirming mainstream media uptake of the Panda/Taub TRE-in-MetS finding."}, {"url": "https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2787246", "title": "JAMA Network Open full article (2787246)", "summary": "JAMA Network Open full-text article on intermittent fasting or related dietary intervention. Peer-reviewed article with a detailed pooled analysis across diabetic and non-diabetic subgroups.
